coredata.dc:title: A degradation trajectory prediction method applicable to various life stages of lithium-ion batteries under complex variable aging conditions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jinghua
Lou, Jiajie
Kainz, Josef
coredata.dc:description: It is imperative to effectively utilize the limited measurement data available at any life stage to address the challenge of predicting the degradation trajectory of lithium-ion batteries under various operating conditions throughout their lifespan. This study presents a method for incorporating base points (points obtained that can be interpolated to predict the degradation trajectories) by applying a convolutional neural network, which learns the mapping relationships from charging data from any stage, even down to a single cycle. The objective is to accurately determine the specific locations of these base points appropriate for interpolation and extrapolation of degradation trajectories. This study utilizes three metaheuristic algorithms to optimize the base point locations and model hyperparameters using different input data to achieve optimal model performance. The proposed method is validated on two independent datasets: A public dataset containing 95 LFP cells and a private dataset comprising 14 NCA cells. The experimental conditions for the NCA test set fluctuate with aging, thereby simulating real-world scenarios. In addition, selection criteria for the input data and specific optimization algorithms are proposed to maximize the potential for achieving optimal accuracy. The results indicate that the minimum RMSE (and MAE) for LFP degradation trajectory predictions can attain 0.67 % (0.51 %) across various base point locations and hyperparameter combinations. These values can improve for the NCA test set to as low as 0.30 % (0.25 %). Furthermore, the optimal strategy in a relatively stable testing environment, such as a laboratory environment, involves the application of the differential evolution (DE) algorithm utilizing a limited number of base points. In addition, the present method is not sensitive to measurement quantity and the life stage at which the input data is located

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is imperative to effectively utilize the limited measurement data available at any life stage to address the challenge of predicting the degradation trajectory of lithium-ion batteries under various operating conditions throughout their lifespan. This study presents a method for incorporating base points (points obtained that can be interpolated to predict the degradation trajectories) by applying a convolutional neural network, which learns the mapping relationships from charging data from any stage, even down to a single cycle. The objective is to accurately determine the specific locations of these base points appropriate for interpolation and extrapolation of degradation trajectories. This study utilizes three metaheuristic algorithms to optimize the base point locations and model hyperparameters using different input data to achieve optimal model performance. The proposed method is validated on two independent datasets: A public dataset containing 95 LFP cells and a private dataset comprising 14 NCA cells. The experimental conditions for the NCA test set fluctuate with aging, thereby simulating real-world scenarios. In addition, selection criteria for the input data and specific optimization algorithms are proposed to maximize the potential for achieving optimal accuracy. The results indicate that the minimum RMSE (and MAE) for LFP degradation trajectory predictions can attain 0.67 % (0.51 %) across various base point locations and hyperparameter combinations. These values can improve for the NCA test set to as low as 0.30 % (0.25 %). Furthermore, the optimal strategy in a relatively stable testing environment, such as a laboratory environment, involves the application of the differential evolution (DE) algorithm utilizing a limited number of base points. In addition, the present method is not sensitive to measurement quantity and the life stage at which the input data is located

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries, which are extensively utilized in contemporary applications [1], exhibit characteristics that result in a decline in their energy delivery capacity with both usage and the passage of time. This phenomenon is referred to as capacity degradation or battery degradation [2]. This poses a challenge for lithium-ion batteries' management throughout their entire lifespan. Battery degradation has diverse causes. Various in-use external factors, such as temperature, charge/discharge rate, etc., have been reported to affect the degradation trajectory of batteries. It has been demonstrated that battery degradation mechanisms can be categorized into five major and thirteen minor interrelated modes [3]. As a consequence, this results in the degradation trajectory exhibiting strong nonlinearities and affecting their prediction with considerable levels of uncertainty. Under this premise, capturing batteries' status continuously becomes extremely problematic [4].

Nevertheless, accurate degradation trajectory prediction facilitates safe operation and optimized utilization of lithium-ion batteries, thus attracting attention. A variety of methods for estimating and predicting battery capacity degradation trends are presented in the existing literature [5]. The models employed can be categorized into two groups: electrochemical models based on lithium-ion batteries' operating principles and data-driven models based on measurement data.

Electrochemical model-based approaches rely on partial differential equations to describe electrochemical reactions inside batteries [6] and combine them with specific degradation mechanisms to predict the battery's degradation tendency. However, such models require detailed knowledge of the degradation mechanism and thus cannot characterize degradation behavior caused by unknown mechanisms [7], which explains the limitations of this approach. Unlike traditional feature-based methods, data-driven methods, which demonstrate potential for contributing to estimating degradation trajectories, are deep learning methods that do not require manually extracted features [8]. This is a consequence of the ineffective generalization capability of manual feature extraction, as evidenced by the fact that the application accuracy of such methods is significantly determined by the operating profile and environment, as well as the cell type [9]. Deep learning can automatically learn hidden features across data, which enables it to reflect degradation behavior produced by unknown mechanisms. The popular deep learning methods applicable to this field are convolutional neural networks (CNN) [10] and recurrent neural networks (RNN) [11]. Li et al. [12] developed a recurrent neural network-based sequence-to-sequence model (RNN-S2S) by incorporating capacity measurements. The model is capable of predicting degradation trends through data from the first 100 cycles as inputs. Lin et al. [13] devised a synthetic dataset generation strategy utilizing polynomial functions, employing synthetic datasets together with CNNs to train degradation trajectory prediction models. Ideally, this will ensure that sufficient prediction accuracy is achievable at an early stage of the entire lifespan.

In recent years, data-driven approaches based on knee points have gained popularity among researchers. The degradation trajectory of a cell can be approximated by two segments [14]: The first part of the lifespan exhibits a virtually linear capacity decrease, commonly referred to as the “slow degradation” phase. The second part of the lifespan suffers a sudden, nearly exponential decrease in capacity, which can be described as the “accelerated degradation” phase. The transition period between these two phases is known as the knee point [15,16]. However, due to the diversity of battery degradation trajectory curves, the knee point definition is not standardized. There are three widely accepted knee point definition categories: the first is the most intuitive, where the tangent to the knee point has a slope equal to the slope of the line joining BoL (begin of life) and EoL (end of life) [9]. The second is based on the intersection of a straight line approximating early decay with the rate of decay after the turning point. This is also commonly recognized as the Bacon-Watts (BW) model [17]. The drawbacks of these two methods are comparable, namely their applicability in complex environments is questionable due to the diversity of degradation trajectory curves. In addition, unconventional trajectory curves may lead to undesirable results for the knee point [18]. The third category consists of methods utilizing machine learning models for online recognition [8,19]. However, they are still subject to different degradation mechanisms while requiring a significant amount of labeled data.

In order to enhance the applicability of the knee-point based approach, the concept of knee-point onset was initially introduced by Fermin-Cueto et al. in the form of a double Bacon-Watts (DBW) model [20]. This concept defines the point that reveals the first signs of a cell's transition to an accelerated degradation phase. It provides an earlier warning about rapid degradation than the knee point. However, these concepts are fundamentally based on categorizing the degradation trajectory into two different phases, “slow degradation” and “accelerated degradation”, which may not be appropriate for all types of battery degradation trajectory curves. For example, Saxena et al. [21] employed the discharge voltage data obtained during the initial 100 cycles to forecast the parameters of the DBW model by applying a CNN. They also evaluated various loss functions of the CNN to achieve optimal accuracy for specific training objectives. Despite these advancements, this method remains contingent upon empirical formulations derived from a significant volume of experimental data, and the robustness of the approach to different battery electrochemical types has yet to be thoroughly examined.

In addition to this, most of the existing degradation trajectory prediction methods are driven by early cycling data and a fixed battery operating environment (i.e., the battery's cycling profile is constant from BoL to EoL). Haris et al. [14] employed a CNN to estimate the parameters of an empirical formula, utilizing data sourced from the initial cycle of the battery's lifespan as input to the model. This method enabled the prediction of remaining useful life (RUL) and degradation trajectories. Nonetheless, the efficacy of this approach for input data from various life stages remains unverified. Strange et al. [22] similarly explored using a single cycle as input for their model. The proposed model accurately predicted EOL and RUL. However, when estimating degradation trajectories, its performance was suboptimal for cells with long lifespans. Chou et al. [23] and Gomez et al. [24] constructed deep learning models based on the DBW model, which facilitated the prediction of the RUL and degradation trajectories. Notably, both studies utilized the same publicly available dataset, and the aging conditions of those batteries were consistently maintained throughout their measurements. Those conditions are quite different from usual application conditions. When the charging profile varies during a battery's life, the original degradation trajectory prediction is no longer valid. Consequently, the prediction of the entire degradation trajectory by data obtained from any cycle in its lifespan is highly desirable for practical applications.

In summary, existing studies have introduced various methodologies for predicting the degradation trajectory of lithium-ion batteries. Nonetheless, these methodologies exhibit certain limitations, which are summarized as follows:
1.
Current approaches do not effectively demonstrate the ability to predict degradation trajectories utilizing data from any stage of the battery lifespan.
2.
The input data requirements for existing predictive models remain substantial. Consequently, there is a need to develop methodologies that can function optimally with a reduced volume of measurement data.
3.
Most current methods have been validated exclusively under stationary charging and discharging conditions.

This study seeks to address these research gaps. Similar to the knee point approach, a curve fitted with multiple base points (points obtained using a data-driven approach that can be interpolated to facilitate the prediction of degradation trajectories) can also effectively represent the degradation trajectory, while avoiding many problems associated with the knee point approach [25]. A classical method of representing curves is piecewise linear interpolation. For certain curves, specific base points selected for interpolation have proven effective in improving accuracy [26,27]. This paper proposes a method that utilizes a limited amount of cycling data (ranging from 1 cycle to 10 cycles) to predict the location of base points that serve as the basis for linear interpolation of the degradation trajectory. This method exploits CNNs' advantages in automatic feature extraction by incorporating various data during charging from different life stages as inputs. It relies on automatically acquired features to predict the base point locations. An accurate prediction of the degradation trajectory will be obtained by interpolating (and extrapolating if necessary) the optimal positions of multiple base points when they are identified. To further enhance the performance of the proposed method, several optimization methods (genetic algorithm (GA), particle swarm optimization (PSO), differential evolution (DE)) are employed for the optimization of the method. In contrast to most previous studies [25,28], the optimization method is not applied only to the hyperparameters of the neural network or only to the base point positions. Packing the hyperparameters and base point locations together for optimization leads to more accuracy in determining the most appropriate degradation trajectory prediction method for different battery types. Such an optimization algorithm-based approach is also free from the limitations imposed by a dedicated model (e.g., BW and DBW).

The proposed methodology is designed to address the identified research gap; its advantages in terms of applicability are evident in two key aspects.
1.
In addition to evaluating the proposed method with the publicly available LFP dataset of Severson et al. [29], a second more comprehensive dataset using NCA batteries was used, which utilized fixed aging conditions for the “training set battery” and aging conditions varying with the aging cycle for the “test set battery”. Chapter 3 illustrates the experimental setup. This further validates the method's ability to generalize to diverse battery usage histories in an environment more comparable to a real-world environment.
2.
The application of the method is not limited to data from the early cycling stages serving as inputs. Cycling data from any stage of lifespan were considered as inputs to evaluate the method's accuracy. As will be shown, it is possible to predict the degradation trajectory under a variable mixture of aging conditions. Furthermore, this study validates scenarios that utilize as low as a single cycle of charging data as input for the model.

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows: Chapter 2 summarizes the structure of the present method and gives a concise description of its individual components. Chapter 3 describes the sources of data employed to test the validity of this method and various details. Chapter 4 presents the performance of the present method under different conditions and then discusses various parameters that may impact accuracy. Chapter 5 summarizes this work and gives an outlook for further research.

The general framework of the proposed method in this paper is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The battery charging capacity, charging current, and equivalent cycle number will be sampled during the charging process. These data are saved as matrices, which facilitates CNN's ability to extract features automatically. With the selected number of base points and the type of optimization algorithm chosen, the positions of individual base points and CNN hyperparameters will be jointly optimized together. The ideal combination provides the most accurate base point locations, based on which interpolation should predict the degradation trajectory. A detailed description of all individual steps of the proposed method will be presented in the subsequent subsections.

The existence of a “knee point” in the lithium-ion battery degradation trajectory is caused by a complex combination of mechanisms such as lithium plating, resistance growth and electrode saturation [30]. In detail, the formation of lithium metal ions on the electrode surface and their deposition during cyclic aging [31], the depletion of the electrolyte and thus loss of active material [32], the rise in internal resistance due to the growth of solid electrolyte interfaces (SEI) and by-products [33] all lead to an accelerated decline in batteries' usable capacity. However, this shift from a slow to an accelerated degradation rate is not abrupt but emerges over multiple cycles. The accurate identification of this transition is therefore challenging.

A classical and easy-to-use method for identifying knee points is the Bacon-Watts model, which expresses the relationship between two straight lines to the left and to the right of an unknown transition point (knee point)

By employing measurement data from the Severson dataset, Fig. 2 demonstrates an example of identification utilizing the BW Model. According to the definition, it is understood that the knee point obtained by BW model represents the midpoint of the entire degradation rate transition phase. This means that the knee point detected by this method may be a delayed warning for the BMS. This is influenced by the nonlinear nature of the degradation trajectory. It increases battery management uncertainty. To address this problem, Fermin-Cueto et al. first proposed the concept of knee onset [20] within the double Bacon-Watts model, which can be expressed as:

represents the knee point. An example of the method is also displayed in Fig. 2. However, as the newest version of the knee point concept, the DBW model cannot completely eliminate the inherent shortcomings of the BW model. Both methods can indicate a sudden accelerated decline in battery capacity after the knee point is reached. The DBW method can also warn that battery capacity decline is about to accelerate. However, in principle, they merely divide the degradation trajectory into two phases, i.e., the “slow degradation” phase and the “accelerated degradation” phase. Such information is relatively crude for the complex operating environment of lithium-ion batteries, especially in cases where the knee point is not obvious.

The BMS operates and manages the battery more efficiently by understanding the degradation trend at different stages of its lifespan. In order to update the information about the degradation trajectory more frequently, this paper employs a prediction method that divides the degradation trajectory into more regions. This prediction method relies on multiple base points. Unlike the BW and DBW models described above, this method involves linear interpolation and – to predict battery degradation - extrapolation of identified base points with SOH decreasing. Specifically, this approach utilizes a neural network model to accurately identify specific base points. Subsequently, it employs linear interpolation techniques to connect these base points, thereby constructing a trajectory that represents battery degradation based on the identified base points. Although linear interpolation may not achieve the level of accuracy provided by other, more complex interpolation methods, it remains a simpler and more stable option for implementation. This method demonstrates enhanced robustness and can effectively mitigate the impact of certain outliers. While alternative interpolation methods may yield superior results, the variations in accuracy are often not substantial. Consequently, when developing a new methodology, it is advantageous to consider the adoption of a more stable interpolation technique.

Fig. 2 depicts an idealized schematic of this approach. With a known starting point of degradation (0 Cycle, 100 % SOH) and a predefined number of base points, methods such as neural networks (NN) are employed to predict the position of each base point on the “equivalent cycle number-SOH” diagram, by incorporating the actual battery measurement data (see 2.2 for details) as input. Depending on a variety of battery types, various operating conditions, etc., the dynamics of battery degradation trajectory can be diverse. This leads to multiple optimal base point positions under different circumstances. Consequently, the optimal base point positions for configuring the degradation trajectory, and the most appropriate NN hyperparameters matching them should be found together by the optimization algorithm. Optimization of the above two sets of parameters simultaneously can improve the accuracy of degradation trajectory reconstruction. This will be explained in more detail in section 2.3.

In sufficiently complex real-world usage environments, with an increasing number of base points, the degradation trend can be expected to be represented more accurately. This will enable the BMS to take further action. Moreover, this approach also combines the inherent advantages of the BW and DBW approach, i.e., early warning of accelerated battery degradation.

Convolutional neural networks have been demonstrated to be remarkably efficient at complex feature extraction. They have the ability to extract features from raw time-series data and thus capture alterations in electrochemical processes as well as battery performance during cycling [35]. In this work, CNN is thus selected to automatically extract features from the input in order to predict the battery degradation trajectory. The most significant difference between CNN and fully connected neural networks is that CNN extracts features by sliding a convolutional kernel over the original data. This is exactly how the convolutional layer works and reduces the number of parameters to train.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the components of our CNN are the input layer, the convolutional layers, the max pooling layer, the flatten layer, and the fully connected layer. The max pooling layer is sandwiched between consecutive convolutional layers. It retains the maximum values of the features in a local region of the input matrix. This compresses the amount of data and parameters, reducing the likelihood of overfitting. The flatten layer, which is typically arranged after the convolutional layers, allows the features after convolutional and max pooling operations to be spread into a one-dimensional vector. This improves the effectiveness of data for model computation. The fully connected layer has all neurons connected with weights, comparable to traditional neural networks. It is commonly placed at the tail of the CNN. In addition, the batch normalization layer (BatchNorm) is applied to the convolutional and max pooling layers. Such a setup stabilizes the distribution of inputs during training (in small batches), yielding more predictive and stable gradient behavior, and leading to an acceleration of the training [36].

Due to the unpredictable discharge behavior of lithium-ion batteries in real-world applications, we capture local patterns from a comparatively controlled charging process. Information from the charging process of any 10 consecutive cycles serves as input, i.e., the charging capacity, the current rate and the equivalent cycle number at each sampling point (a complete charge and discharge is defined as an equivalent cycle). The CNN proposed in this work consists of two 2D convolutional layers, a max pooling layer, a flatten layer and three fully connected layers.

All of the above 3 inputs were sampled for 100 values during each of the 10 charging cycles, resulting in an input sample size of 10*100 per input. When utilizing data from 10 consecutive charging processes as input, the input size of the model is represented as 3*10*100. This configuration involves three features ( Q , Cycle , CRate ) multiplied by 10 charging processes, each comprising 100 sampling values. Due to the intention of limiting the first convolution to the interior of each individual charging process, the convolution kernel was not a square kernel. Instead, a 1*10 rectangular kernel was employed. A larger non-overlapping step size of 10 was selected for the first convolution, reducing the input sample size to 10*10. The implementation of a non-overlapping kernel reduces the number of weights in subsequent layers. In general, simpler networks tend to generalize, computationally faster, and learn from fewer training samples [37]. The size of the max pooling layer kernel and the second convolutional kernel are 2*2 and 2*2 (when employing data from a single charging process as input, the dimensions are 1*2.) with a step size of 1 and 1. This extracts features and reduces data dimensionality. The ReLU activation function is deployed on all convolutional and max pooling layers to simplify computation and ability to prevent gradient vanishing [38]. The flatten layer and the three fully connected layers employ the Sigmoid activation function. The multiple fully connected layers provide enhanced nonlinear expressivity. This can be exploited to learn complex patterns and relationships when model complexity is considerable, improving model accuracy and generalization. The final output of the model delineates the position information of all base points, which can be employed for interpolation to predict the degradation trajectory.

It is pertinent to highlight that this study focused exclusively on data derived from 1, 5, or 10 consecutive charging processes as inputs. The decision to refrain from examining more extensive data, such as that comprising 50 or even 100 charging processes, is predicated on the fact that analyzing 50 or 100 charging processes would represent a driving distance of approximately 20000 or 40000 kilometers, respectively, given the extended range of modern electric vehicles. Gathering data from such a substantial number of usage histories to predict the aging trajectory would considerably constrain the applicability of the proposed methodology, rendering it impractical.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization algorithms for joint optimization of hyperparameters and base point positions
ce:section.ce:para: A crucial role in improving machine learning models' accuracy is played by hyperparameter tuning, i.e., by the process of selecting optimal hyperparameter values. They are not learned from the data but are predetermined prior to the training process. By fine-tuning them, the model's performance and accuracy will be enhanced since neural networks are generally remarkably sensitive to the correct hyperparameter configuration. Similarly, since the degradation trajectory exhibited by diverse battery types under varied operating conditions is extremely variable, the location of the base point with excellent generalization capability cannot be chosen the same for every application. The performance of the method proposed in this paper is also heavily dependent on the position of the base point. Therefore, applying optimization algorithms is highly advantageous for identifying suitable model hyperparameters and determining optimal base point locations specifically aligned with various battery usage environments.

This paper addresses the joint optimization of model hyperparameters and base point positions. This strategy effectively expands the parameter space for all parameters involved in the optimization process. In scenarios where only hyperparameters or base point positions are optimized independently, the parameter space becomes restricted, as one of these elements is fixed as a constant. Consequently, our approach to joint optimization offers a significant advantage by enhancing the performance of the proposed method through a more expansive parameter space.

Metaheuristics are a class of algorithms for solving complex optimization problems. Their core idea is to approximate the optimal solution incrementally by searching the problem space. Metaheuristic algorithms can handle complex, nonlinear problems and require no assumptions about the specific model of the problem. This class of algorithms has been widely implemented in hyperparametric optimization problems for machine learning [39]. Its generic process can be expressed as [40]:
1.
Population and iteration termination condition initialization. Population initialization requires arbitrary solution selection. The fitness values of the candidate solutions will be computed according to an objective function and sequentially ranked.
2.
New candidate solutions are generated through an algorithmic mechanism. The fitness values of all candidate solutions should likewise be revised, candidate solutions should be sorted accordingly.
3.
Identify the candidate solution containing the best fitness value, check the termination criteria.

In this work, typical representatives of metaheuristic algorithms, i.e. genetic algorithm (GA), particle swarm optimization (PSO), and differential evolutionary algorithm (DE) are employed for the joint optimization of CNN hyperparameters and base point locations, respectively. The selection of these three algorithms is grounded in their extensive application within the field of lithium-ion batteries. They have been particularly effective in optimizing the hyperparameters and structural components of models designed to estimate the battery's state [41–43]. Furthermore, PSO has demonstrated notably high performance in accurately identifying the parameters of equivalent circuit models [44].

The combination of algorithm and number of base points with the most efficient performance applicable to a particular type of specific operating condition will be determined. The combination with the most impressive generalization performance will similarly be discussed. Table 1 explains the details of the parameters to be optimized and their range. Specifically, the objective function employed in the optimization algorithm for this study focuses on minimizing the (RMSE) error between the predicted and actual degradation trajectories. The predicted trajectories are derived through the interpolation method outlined in Section 2.1, while the optimization algorithm is designed to minimize this error effectively. The optimization algorithm will iteratively execute the CNN framework based on the range of parameter values outlined in Table 1. This process aims to identify the most suitable individual parameters for the dataset utilized.

Although the optimization algorithms cannot be directly compared due to their unique hyperparameters, efforts were made to ensure a fair comparison among them. To this end, a population size of 30 was established for all three algorithms, each executed for 15 iterations. Furthermore, the hyperparameters specific to each algorithm were selected based on the recommended values provided by Matlab.

The utilization of measurement data under diverse operating conditions facilitates checking the generalizability of the proposed method. The open-source dataset provided by Severson et al. [29] was selected for its extensive application of multiple charging strategies. However, as mentioned in Section 1, all existing open-source datasets known to the authors implement a constant charging strategy from BoL to EoL for a given cell. This assumption is beneficial for method development and neural network modeling. Nevertheless, this ideal situation is relatively dissimilar to lithium-ion batteries' operating environment in real-world applications. In the absence of datasets on the charging condition variation with battery degradation, this study also investigated 14 cylindrical 18,650 batteries aged at a controlled 25 °C, similar to our previous work [43]. Detailed information will be presented in 3.1.2.

The first dataset analyzed was generated from 124 commercial LFP batteries. These are cylindrical batteries manufactured by A123 Systems with a nominal capacity of 1.1 Ah. All batteries were subjected to charge/discharge at a constant 30 °C temperature until EoL was reached. The discharge conditions remained identical for all batteries, i.e., CC-CV (constant current-constant voltage) discharge at 4C. However, the charging conditions were diverse, i.e., charged to 80 % SOC with 72 different one-step or two-step charging approaches, followed by CC-CV charging at 1C. Voltage, current, temperature and internal resistance data were continuously recorded during the experiment. More detailed information on this dataset is available at https://data.matr.io/1.

In this batch of batteries, there is considerable variation in the equivalent cycle number experienced. It results from inconsistent aging rates caused by differing charging strategies. The amount of batteries with an insufficient or excessive number of cycles is relatively low. To maintain sample availability for CNN construction as well as minimize the effect of so-called “outliers”, 95 batteries with an equivalent cycle number between 500 and 1200 were considered in this work. The batteries were categorized into different groups that experienced 500, 600, …, 1100 cycles. A cell was randomly selected from each group separately. This resulted in 7 cells as the “test set”. The remaining 88 cells were divided into training and validation sets in an approximate ratio of 9:1. Fig. 3a illustrates the degradation trajectories of the selected batteries.

The second dataset utilized was generated from 14 commercial NCA batteries. These cylindrical 18,650 batteries were manufactured by Panasonic with a nominal capacity of 3.35 Ah. All measurements were performed at 25 °C. Considering the function of these cells during degradation trajectory prediction, they were categorized into “training set” cells (9 cells) and “test set” cells (5 cells). The aging conditions of each particular cell in the training set remained unchanged over their lifespan. 3 levels of charging rate and 3 levels of DOD (depth of discharge) were implemented in these 9 cells, which were designed to reflect generally the effects of various mechanisms on degradation due to diverse environmental conditions. CC-CV was employed for all charging/discharging processes during experiments. The experiment was terminated until the SOH approached 80 %. Table 2 summarizes the aging conditions of the training set cells.

Table 3 presents the aging conditions of cells belonging to the test set. In order to investigate the performance of the proposed method under conditions comparable to the real-world environment (i.e., charging conditions fluctuate during cell operation), variable tests (with changing aging conditions) were performed utilizing separate cells independent of the training set. The aging conditions corresponding to each training set cell were subsequently applied to cell #10 for 42 equivalent cycles (except for Test 6 with 1C/100 %DoD, which leads to excessive aging). Based on the conditions established for cell #10, cells #11, #12, and #13 had further modified charging rates, adjusted DoD, and altered aging cycles. The revised parameters are highlighted in bold and underlined in Table 3. Compared to the training set cells, cell #14 was expected to undergo completely different conditions. Such a setup can definitively check CNN for the overfitting phenomenon. The applicability of the proposed method to complex environments can be evaluated as well. Fig. 3b illustrates the degradation trajectory of the second dataset battery.

In contrast to the LFP dataset, choosing a maximum amount of training data was not a reasonable choice for the NCA dataset due to the “variable aging conditions” of the NCA test set, which differ from fixed aging conditions in the training set. Consequently, we cannot transfer batteries between the test and training sets. Such a practice would substantially diminish the challenge posed to the model in predicting degradation trajectories, thereby undermining our ability to effectively assess the applicability of the proposed method.

Valid measurements obtained during battery charging, such as voltage, current, charging capacity, and equivalent cycle number, contribute to track the capacity degradation and thus for health management [45]. Based on this premise, the three measurements of charging capacity, charging rate, and equivalent cycle number during the charging period (correlates with depth of discharge, DoD), are incorporated as inputs for identifying the optimal base point location via CNN. In this work, the above inputs are sampled at 100 points during each charging period. Specifically, after obtaining initial and final values of the voltages during the charging period, the input data described above are taken at 100 voltages spread equidistantly between them. If the resolution of the measurement data is insufficient, interpolation is employed to compensate for the missing data. Outliers were removed from the measurement data and noise filtering was applied before processing.

All input data must be stored in the form of a matrix. Take charging capacity as an example:

With this treatment, the three inputs implicitly represent the battery charging process over a certain period of time. Consequently, the inputs to the model are determined to be X =

Furthermore, in all scenarios presented in this manuscript, the input lengths utilized for training and testing are the same. Specifically, when data from a single charging process is employed for model training, data from only one charging process is utilized for testing. This methodology is adopted to enhance the stability and reliability of the model in practical applications.

To assess the performance of the proposed method, the RMSE (root mean squared error) and MAE (mean absolute error) between the predicted degradation trajectories and the measured degradation trajectories are employed to analyze prediction accuracy. These error measures are expressed as:

In order to clarify the method's applicability at various stages throughout the lifespan, the measured data at different SOH stages can be chosen as inputs. For the LFP dataset, measurement data were acquired and used as inputs for all cells at [99 % 95 % 91 % 87 %] SOH, both for the training set and test set. For the NCA dataset, the training set cells collected data at [99 % 97 % 95 % 93 % 91 % 89 % 87 % 85 %] SOH. The aging conditions of the test set cells fluctuate dynamically. Each combination of aging conditions lasted only 42 equivalent cycles. Therefore, measurement data were exclusively acquired at the initial stage of each combination. All the above inputs collected at different SOHs will be applied as inputs to the CNN one by one to obtain the corresponding prediction results respectively. It is worth mentioning that the data at 83 % SOH have not been considered in either the LFP dataset or the NCA dataset. This is because predicting the entire degradation curve near the end of its lifespan is not required for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction results for the LFP dataset
ce:section.ce:para: The training set of the LFP dataset consists of 88 cells with total cycles in the range from 500 to 1200, while the test set is composed of 7 randomly selected cells with cycles in the range of 500, 600, …, 1100 (test cells #1 to #7). In order to verify the impact of various parameter combinations on the method performance, multiple combinations consisting of a total of 3 optimization algorithms, 2 base point numbers, and 2 numbers of cycles utilized as inputs are examined. These combinations will be analyzed for 4 different SOHs (where input data is obtained). Considering the large data amount in the LFP dataset even more levels of parameters could not be examined exhaustively. However, the selected scope of parameters is indeed quite representative.

Fig. 4 illustrates the best case, the average case, and the worst case for all of the above combinations and for every test set cell. The results presented are derived from using a consistent test set for training purposes. The variations in prediction results can be attributed to a range of factors, including the selection of optimization algorithms, variations in the lengths of input data, the number of base points employed, distinct model hyperparameters, and the various stages of battery life (or SOH) at which the input data are recorded. RMSE is employed as a selection criterion. In the worst case, most cells maintain MAE levels around 3 %-points. This is acceptable considering that the analysis in Fig. 4 involves a scenario where only limited information (exactly one cycle) is used as model input. For the least effective test cell, #4, the MAE reaches 6 %-points. It overestimates battery degradation rates, but its prediction of the degradation trend is nevertheless not a safety risk. The more problematic scenarios for the BMS are instead test cells #1, #2 and #3. Although the error value is not large, the underestimation of the degradation rate in the second half of the lifespan may pose a safety hazard in operation. This could be improved in subsequent work by modifying the loss function of the proposed method. An example is the “2ndHalfCapLoss” loss function mentioned in Sexena's work [21], which solely takes into account prediction errors in the second half of their lifespan. In the average case, the method demonstrates the ability to accurately track the degradation trajectory of all cells. Similarly, however, for test cells #2 and #6, the predicted degradation trajectories indicate a significant underestimation of the degradation rate at the end of the lifespan. Again, this could be improved by the selection of a modified loss function. In contrast, the best case predicted by the present method for individual cells is encouraging. All predictions are extremely reliable. A dangerous underestimation of degradation rates at any stage of lifespan never appears. RMSEs (MAEs) as low as 0.51 % (0.34 %) and as high as 0.95 % (0.79 %) are undoubtedly a solid guarantee of the BMS' efficiency. Therefore, it is necessary to analyze the parameter combinations corresponding to these cases subsequently.

The average RMSE and the average MAE of the degradation trajectories of all cells corresponding to the levels of each of the above-mentioned parameters are presented in Table 4 . For all parameter combinations, optimal accuracy is obtained utilizing GA with 4 base points and data from 10 cycles as input. However, contrary to the assumptions, increasing the base point number does not necessarily result in improved model efficiency. Overall, 4 base points deliver comparable performance to 5 base points and have an advantage in computational resource consumption. Regarding the number of cycles employed as inputs, fewer cycles result in a decrease in accuracy. However, this deterioration is not unacceptable and may be outweighed by the improved applicability of the method because fewer inputs are required. But real-world data may place higher demands on the model than laboratory measurements. Therefore, consideration of such a trade-off is still required by the BMS in actual operations depending on the situation.

Fig. 5 expresses the difference between various optimization algorithms more clearly. PSO's relative inefficiency can be clearly seen. In the most extreme case, i.e. when selecting 5 base points, the average RMSE (MAE) provided by PSO is about 40 % larger than for the other algorithms. Although the performance of PSO is comparable to its competitors in the case of 4 base points, its reduced stability is demonstrated by the fact that the model quality deteriorates for 5 base points. In either case, DE and GA perform approximately equally. GA provides slightly higher accuracy than DE. However, considering the limitations of GA in terms of convergence speed [46], DE should be considered the preferred approach for the present method. This decision is supported by the quantitative analysis provided in Table 5 . The table details the training duration, average GPU utilization, and average memory utilization associated with various algorithms employing 10 cycles as input. All model training was executed on a computer equipped with an RTX3060 graphics card and 16 GB of RAM, ensuring the data are directly comparable across different scenarios. Although the models demonstrate similar accuracy, DE consistently requires less training time than GA across all scenarios, displaying comparable computational resource utilization. While PSO achieves a faster training completion, its corresponding reduction in model accuracy mitigates the advantage of this speed. This observation highlights its susceptibility to a local optimal. In addition to this, the impact of the initial SOH within the input data is depicted as well in Fig. 5. It can be ascertained that the data trend is relatively smooth except for the fluctuation of PSO at 95 % SOH. This demonstrates that the SOH in which the input data are located does not have a decisive impact on model accuracy. In this way, we can ascertain the robustness of the present method. Measuring data and applying the present method at any stage of lifespan can provide a reliable prediction of the degradation trajectory.

In summary, the proposed method can effectively predict the degradation trajectory of LFP cells. A generic and well-functioning combination of parameters is the DE algorithm with 4 base points (fewer base points). The number of cycles (i.e., length) of the input data should be selected according to the actual situation. This is due to the fact that, while fewer base points and shortened input lengths do not result in a reduction in model performance, the reason could be that the laboratory measurement environment is relatively stable compared to the actual battery operation environment. This reduces the requirements for the model, however complex environments might demand more base points and extended input lengths. This requires further validation with real operation data. Furthermore, the structure of CNNs may not be ideal for managing multiple cycle-stacked input configurations. The negative influence on accuracy associated with this model architecture has the potential to, in certain circumstances, negate the advantages provided by large inputs. In specific situations where input data is readily accessible, computational resources are sufficient, and there are stringent accuracy requirements, the CNN + LSTM architecture may present a promising alternative, as it is more adept at handling multiple cycle-stacked structures. Finally, the proposed method is insensitive to the battery aging stage where the input data is located.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction results for the NCA dataset
ce:section.ce:para: The training set of the NCA data consists of 9 cells (#1 to #9) that cyclically age to EoL under 9 fixed conditions, while the test set includes 5 cells (#10 to #14) that perform the variable aging test in Section 3.1.2. Due to the more manageable size of the NCA dataset compared to the LFP dataset, the computational resources required for validation of multiple parameter combinations are reduced. Therefore, a broad range of combinations are examined here. If the variation of a certain parameter has a comparable effect on the degradation trajectories of NCA and LFP cells, it is plausible that this will also be similar for other cell types. Therefore, 3 optimization algorithms, 3 distinct numbers of base points, and 3 numbers of cycles utilized as inputs will be investigated in conjunction with the NCA dataset.

The degradation trajectory prediction for the NCA test set is illustrated in Fig. 6 . The aging conditions of those cells are variable. The developed model can predict the complete degradation trajectory under a fixed aging condition. Therefore, to generate predictions for varying aging conditions, it is essential to utilize data that is specific to each condition in order to forecast its entire degradation trajectory. Following this process, we can extract the pertinent segments from the predicted data. In order to determine the degradation trajectories in this case, measurement data from the initial stage of each aging condition are employed as input. Notably, the predefined input data's length remains uniform across all aging conditions. The entire degradation trajectories are then calculated piecewise for the different aging conditions. To give an example, the complete degradation trajectory of a certain test cell under aging condition #1 will be determined from the initial cycle data. The first 42 cycles of this predicted degradation trajectory are used as the first segment of the trajectory for this cell in Fig. 6. The estimated SOH value for the 42nd cycle under condition #1 will be taken as the starting point for the prediction under aging condition #2. The identical operation will be repeated for aging conditions #2 through #10. The piecewisely calculated segments together form the predicted trajectories in Fig. 6.

One conclusion obvious from Fig. 6 is that the prediction accuracy of the NCA dataset outperforms the LFP dataset, despite having similar parameter combinations. One potential explanation is as follows. Since the NCA test cells have multiple aging conditions applied, the DoD (depth of discharge) under these aging conditions is not consistently 100 %. Therefore the actual capacity is not readily available during cyclic aging. The solution adopted in this work is to perform a capacity measurement at the last cycle corresponding to each aging condition. This is done to obtain the SOH associated with a specific cycle number. The SOHs for the cycles for which no capacity measurement is available are then determined by linear interpolation. Since the proposed method employs linear interpolation based on base point locations, this may have reduced prediction error partially. As a result the NCA dataset obtains attractive error values. However, it is crucial to point out that despite these facts, the errors presented in Fig. 6 are reasonable values.

Compared to the LFP dataset, there are considerably fewer cases of underestimates of degradation rates in the “worst case” category of the NCA dataset. Model deviations of that type only appear for cell #10 in early life and cell #14 in the late stage. Another possibility exists in variations caused by cell type. A well-known characteristic of LFP cells is their relatively flat capacity-voltage (Q-U) curve. Since this work employs voltage-equidistant sampling, the impact of measurement noise and measurement accuracy is more significant on LFP cells' flat Q-U curves. This makes sampling LFP cells more challenging. In contrast, the steep Q-U curve of NCA cells avoids erroneous sampling. This leads to enhanced applicability and robustness of the proposed method for NCA cells. For the average case, the underestimation of the degradation rate is further minimized while the overall accuracy is improved compared to the worst case. A marginal underestimation of the degradation rate exists in the early life of cell #10. However, an overall prediction accuracy of less than 1 % MAE is successfully achieved for the degradation trajectory. This is currently the relative lowest value for prediction accuracy across cells in the average case. The best case for the NCA dataset demonstrates extremely reliable prediction. The degradation rate is almost never underestimated. The fluctuations in degradation trends due to various aging conditions are always tracked accurately. The RMSE (MAE) of the 5 test cells up to a minimum of 0.15 % (0.12 %) and a maximum remaining of 0.58 % (0.48 %) is impressive. As previously discussed in subsection 4.1, we also attempt now to identify the most appropriate parameter combination that reproduces the “best case” for the NCA dataset.

Table 6 details the impact of all 3 levels of each parameter on prediction accuracy, respectively. The strengths and weaknesses of optimization algorithms exhibit similarities to the LFP dataset: again, PSO is comparatively an inferior choice. However, for the NCA dataset, the performance drawback of PSO is not as pronounced as for the LFP dataset. In most cases, the accuracy delivered by PSO is just marginally inferior to that of DE and GA. In addition, similarly to the previous findings, DE and GA perform equally well. The accuracy they demonstrate is comparable in almost all cases, which makes DE the preferred choice thanks to its superiority in convergence speed. Based on the quantitative analysis of computational duration and resources presented in Table 7 , the speed advantage of PSO becomes increasingly evident in comparison to the LFP dataset. Furthermore, the disparity in prediction accuracy for PSO when applied to the NCA test set is not as pronounced as with the LFP test set. Thus, it is crucial to consider the trade-off between prediction accuracy and computational duration in specific circumstances. Accordingly, PSO may serve as a viable option when computational resources are significantly limited and the prioritization of computational time is essential. An explanation of the effect of base point numbers and the number of cycles applied as inputs is presented in Fig. 7 . It can be concluded that this time an increase in base point number cannot produce an effective enhancement in model accuracy. Therefore, a relatively limited base point number is generally favored for BMS applications due to benefits in computational speed. From the perspective of the input data length, the conclusions are consistent with the results for the LFP dataset. This makes the application of prolonged data lengths not a mandatory option. The BMS should select based on the actual application. As with the problems associated with the LFP dataset, although the NCA dataset applies a more complex measurement environment, this is nonetheless a relatively stable laboratory measurement compared to actual operating conditions. The advantage of the limited number of base points and reduced data lengths may exist in that laboratory data are inherently less demanding to model. However, the model may benefit from the help of a larger number of base points and increased data lengths when confronted with complex real-world data. This requires further validation afterward. This is because an increase in the number of base points will yield benefits in more challenging test environments, where the degradation trajectory of the battery is often less predictable and exhibits more significant variability. While we have imposed complex variable aging conditions on the test set, it is essential to note that these conditions remain within the context of laboratory testing, which is typically regarded as an ideal scenario. Consequently, a reduced number of base points can still achieve high accuracy. A greater number of base points does not provide significant advantages; instead, it introduces additional randomness and consumes more computational resources, diminishing effectiveness in more stable testing situations. Nonetheless, when the methodology is applied in actual vehicle settings, it has to keep the potential to accommodate more base points when confronted with real-world measurement data. We hypothesize that the increase in accuracy associated with additional base points becomes negligible once an optimal number is reached. Beyond this point, further increases may induce fluctuations attributable to various influencing factors, including test errors, measurement noise, or uncertainties inherent in deep learning. Additionally, increasing the number of base points makes the optimization algorithm's task harder, increasing the likelihood of getting stuck in a local optimum. Therefore, the optimal number of base points—representing the balance between computational resources and model accuracy—will vary across different testing environments.

In total, the aging prediction with the proposed method is remarkably accurate for NCA cells. The generalized optimal parameter combination for NCA cells is the DE algorithm, where the number of base points in the investigated range has little impact on model quality for our measurement database. Similarly, the number of cycles for the input data is of little consequence. In summary, the parameter combinations performing well for LFP cells are effective for NCA cells, too. It is reasonable to assume that this combination should be applicable to other cell types as well.

In addition to a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method's performance, it is also rigorously compared with the existing baseline method to more effectively demonstrate the advancement of the proposed approach. Detailed information is available in Appendix A.

Accurate prediction of the battery degradation trajectory based on a limited amount of measurements collected during operation is critical for advancing battery technology, optimizing charging strategies, etc. The prediction of the degradation trajectory provides an effective indicator of the battery's lifespan. Therefore, the method can contribute to the dynamic optimization of battery applications. This study proposes a CNN-based prediction model based on base points for linear interpolation of the degradation trajectory. This approach, without relying on a specific model (e.g., BW/DBW), can utilize a minimal amount of measurement data as low as one cycle collected from any life stage to predict the location of base points automatically with CNN. With the known initial point of the degradation trajectory (0 Cycle, 100 % SOH), the base points are further interpolated and extrapolated to ultimately obtain an accurate estimate. The proposed method is validated with both a publicly available LFP dataset and an NCA dataset generated from additional experiments. The NCA test set incorporates a variable aging method that, to the extent of the authors' knowledge, does not appear in existing literature. Given the significant impact of the neural network hyperparameters on the model's performance, a joint optimization of hyperparameters and base point positions is performed in this work. This optimization is performed by three distinct metaheuristic algorithms: DE (differential evolutionary algorithm), GA (genetic algorithm), and PSO (particle swarm optimization). Overall, we introduce a base point concept in degradation trajectory modeling and an original approach to the joint optimization of base point locations and model architecture, this work integrates and tailors various standard methods in a novel predictive framework.

Prior to implementation, the proposed method requires determining the type of optimization algorithm, the total number of base points, and the number of cycles to be employed as inputs. The corresponding accuracy values for various combinations of the above parameters are presented for both the LFP dataset and the NCA dataset. We present these results as “best case”, “average case” and “worst case” according to obtained RMSE magnitude. The results demonstrate that the average RMSEs (MAEs) of the three scenarios for the LFP dataset are 0.67 % (0.51 %), 1.97 % (1.22 %), and 5.04 % (3.11 %), respectively. The corresponding values for NCA are 0.30 % (0.25 %), 0.61 % (0.51 %), and 1.00 % (0.80 %). The variations in accuracy obtained for diverse combinations reveal the sensitivity of neural network training results to these parameters. In order to ensure that the “best case” scenario largely occurs in practical applications, this work presents a general parameter selection recommendation suitable for both LFP and NCA cells. This recommended parameter combination is the DE algorithm with a limited number of base points within a certain range (3 or 4 base points for the data involved in this work). The number of cycles selected as input depends case-by-case, since the enhancement from extra data is not significant. However, the more stable measurement environment of laboratory measurements relative to real-world conditions poses a less stringent challenge to the data quality, which makes it worthwhile to further investigate whether fewer data are still favorable for real-world data. Considering that conclusions derived for both LFP and NCA cells are identical, we anticipate that this approach is generalizable to other cell types. In addition, this method is not sensitive to the aging state in which the measurement data are acquired.

The proposed data-driven framework offers a degradation trajectory prediction solution for all life stages of lithium-ion batteries. This framework is validated by a complex mixture of aging measurements comparable to actual battery application conditions. This validation methodology imposes a considerable requirement for the performance testing of the proposed method. Furthermore, the method exhibits satisfactory performance under various conditions, including varying input sequence lengths, different stages of battery life where input data is located, differing numbers of base points, and multiple optimization algorithms. This underscores the robustness of the proposed approach. Additionally, as elaborated in Appendix A, a comparative analysis between the proposed method and state-of-the-art techniques affirms its effectiveness. It highlights its significant advantages over existing methodologies. The proposed methodology facilitates BMS management over the entire lifespan and provides valuable insights for next-generation intelligent battery management systems.

Although the proposed method has been validated across diverse scenarios, several challenges must be addressed before its practical application to electric vehicles can be realized. These challenges include managing the effects of fluctuating temperature conditions during real-world operations and addressing the inconsistency of cells in battery packs. Furthermore, integrating the proposed method with incremental capacity analysis and long and short-term memory networks represents a promising avenue for future development. These areas will be the primary focus of our efforts in the next phase of our research.

Jinghua Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiajie Lou: Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Josef Kainz: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method with the baseline method
ce:section.ce:para: To effectively demonstrate the proposed method's advantages compared to existing methodologies, particularly in scenarios characterized by insufficient input data or data derived from various battery life stages, we conducted a comparative analysis with a baseline method. This baseline approach relies on predicting degradation trajectories through an empirical formulation known as the DBW model (referenced in Eq. 2). The same CNN network employed in the main text was utilized to predict the individual parameters of the DBW model. The model's input was determined to consist of measurement data from 10 consecutive charging processes, given that most existing studies are anchored in the availability of ample input data. Following the validation process utilized for the proposed method, both LFP and NCA datasets were employed to validate the baseline model. The division of training and test sets was executed in the same manner as previously established in the prior validation.

The prediction results from the baseline method are presented in Fig. A.1. Similar to the validation process of the proposed method. We utilized three different parameter optimization techniques and four distinct life stages of input data to validate the model's accuracy.
Fig. A.1
Results of the degradation trajectory prediction under different conditions (optimization algorithms, SOH corresponding to input cycles) for LFP batteries with 10 input cycles: best case, average case and worst case.
Fig. A.1

One notable conclusion is that the gap between the baseline method and the proposed method in the best-case scenario is not substantial; in fact, there is one instance where the baseline method achieves better accuracy than the proposed method. However, in the average case, this gap becomes more pronounced. This discrepancy partially arises because the empirical formulas rely on an input length of 10 cycles, while the proposed method includes scenarios where only one cycle of data is used as input. This makes the empirical formula appear more advantageous in the best-case comparison. Conversely, the input data is not always at the early stage of its life—a common assumption in existing studies. Thus, the significant decline in the accuracy of the empirical formula-based method in average cases can be attributed to this misalignment. This indicates that data from different life stages do not consistently favor the empirical formula approach. A more detailed analysis can be found in Fig. A.2.
Fig. A.2
Analysis of parameters affecting model accuracy for LFP batteries: SOH corresponding to input cycles.
Fig. A.2

The accuracy of the empirical formula method is excellent when input data is from the initial stages of the battery's life. However, the performance is inconsistent when using data from various life stages. As the SOH decreases, accuracy declines sharply and then gradually recovers, but it never returns to the level achieved when using data from the initial stage. One possible explanation is that the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) film does not form stably during the initial stages of the cell's life, meaning the empirical equations based on measurement data during this time depend on this unstable electrochemical state. Once the battery has been utilized for a specified duration and the SEI film has achieved stability, the effectiveness of the empirical formula method becomes insufficient. In contrast, the results of the proposed method, as discussed in the main text, demonstrate significant advantages.

To compare the proposed method's performance against that of the baseline method when utilizing a single cycle as input, we validated the empirical formula-based approach under conditions involving a limited dataset. The results of this evaluation are presented in Table A.1. A significant observation is that the robustness of the proposed method is remarkably superior to that of the baseline method. Specifically, applying the proposed method yields a prediction accuracy of the LFP test set that exhibits minimal variation across different input data lengths. Conversely, while the baseline method achieves marginally lower accuracy than the proposed method when provided with adequate input data, its accuracy deteriorates considerably when the input data is constrained. These findings highlight the superior efficacy of the proposed method, particularly in scenarios where the input data is limited.
Table A.1
Comparison of prediction accuracy using different lengths of input data.

Given that empirical formula-based methods require ten cycles of data as input, and that the life stage of the input data remains constant for each prediction segment within the NCA dataset, only three prediction scenarios exist for each cell when utilizing the three distinct optimization algorithms. These scenarios are depicted in Fig. A.3. It is apparent that, despite the empirical formula-based method consistently relying on ten cycles of measurement data, the proposed method (which incorporates scenarios with 5 and 1 cycles) demonstrates superior prediction accuracy. This distinction is evident in both best-case and average-case scenarios. This conclusion aligns with observations made regarding the LFP dataset, indicating that empirical formula-based methods are susceptible to the life stage of the measured data. This sensitivity constitutes a considerable disadvantage for empirical methods and underscores the advantages associated with the proposed approach. Additional validation using the NCA dataset is presented in Table A.1, which illustrates the prediction accuracy of the baseline method when relying solely on one cycle as input. Similar to findings from the LFP dataset, the accuracy of the baseline method significantly diminishes when the input data is limited, whereas the proposed method exhibits greater robustness and applicability.
